title: Headache Self-Care
question: What can I do for a headache?
answer: Mild headaches are often helped by rest, fluids, regular meals, reducing bright light, and avoiding alcohol. If headaches are new, changing, frequent, or interfering with daily life, it is worth checking in with a clinician.
self_care: Drink water or an oral rehydration drink if you may be dehydrated.
Rest in a quiet, dim room.
Eat something light if you skipped a meal.
Avoid taking pain relievers more often than the label or your clinician recommends.
monitor: Fever, neck stiffness, vision change, weakness, confusion
red_flags: Headache with stiff neck, fever, confusion, fainting, weakness, vision change, or trouble speaking

title: Fever Basics
question: What should I know about fever?
answer: A fever is usually a sign that the body is responding to an illness or infection. Rest, fluids, and temperature monitoring can help while you watch for worsening symptoms.
self_care: Drink enough fluids.
Rest and avoid overheating.
Use fever medicine only as directed on the label or by a clinician.
Avoid aspirin for children or teenagers unless a clinician specifically says to use it.
monitor: Temperature and how long fever has lasted
Rash, stiff neck, confusion, dehydration, breathing trouble
Age, pregnancy, immune problems, or serious chronic conditions
red_flags: Fever that is very high or lasts several days

title: Cough and Cold Support
question: How can I manage a cough or cold?
answer: Many coughs and colds improve with rest, fluids, humidified air, and avoiding smoke. A cough that worsens, lasts longer than expected, or comes with breathing trouble needs medical guidance.
self_care: Drink fluids.
Use humidified air or steam from a warm shower.
Avoid smoke and strong odors.
Consider honey for cough if age is over 1 year.

title: Dehydration Warning Signs
question: How do I know if I might be dehydrated?
answer: Possible dehydration signs include thirst, dry mouth, darker urine, dizziness, fatigue, and urinating less often. Dehydration can become serious quickly in babies, older adults, and people with vomiting or diarrhea.
self_care: Sip water or an oral rehydration drink.
Take small frequent sips if nauseated.
Avoid alcohol.
Seek care if fluids will not stay down.
red_flags: Signs of dehydration in a baby or older adult

title: Diabetes and Blood Sugar Awareness
question: What are general blood sugar warning signs?
answer: Blood sugar that is too low may cause shakiness, sweating, hunger, confusion, or weakness. Blood sugar that is too high may cause thirst, frequent urination, blurry vision, or fatigue. People with diabetes should follow their care plan and contact their care team for repeated unusual readings.
self_care: Follow your diabetes care plan.
Keep glucose supplies available if your clinician advised them.
Record readings, symptoms, meals, and medicines.
Stay hydrated during illness unless your clinician told you to restrict fluids.
monitor: Ketones if your plan includes checking them
red_flags: Very high readings with ketones or severe symptoms

title: High Blood Pressure Check-In
question: What should I know about high blood pressure?
answer: High blood pressure often has no symptoms, so home readings and routine care matter. A single high reading can happen, but repeated high readings or symptoms should be discussed with a clinician.
self_care: Rest quietly for five minutes before taking a reading.
Use the cuff size recommended for your arm.
Record readings with date and time.
Take prescribed blood pressure medicines as directed.
monitor: Chest pain, shortness of breath, weakness, severe headache
red_flags: Severe headache with confusion or vision changes

title: Asthma and Wheezing
question: What should I do about wheezing or asthma symptoms?
answer: Wheezing, chest tightness, coughing, or shortness of breath can happen with asthma or other breathing problems. Follow your asthma action plan if you have one, and seek urgent help for severe breathing trouble.
self_care: Use prescribed rescue medicine according to your action plan.
Move away from smoke, dust, cold air, or other triggers.
Sit upright and try to stay calm.
Contact your care team if rescue medicine is needed more often than usual.
monitor: Breathing rate and ability to speak
Peak flow if you use one

title: Allergy and Rash Triage
question: How should I think about allergies or rash?
answer: Allergies may cause sneezing, itching, hives, rash, watery eyes, or swelling. Mild symptoms can often be managed by avoiding triggers, but swelling of the face or throat or breathing trouble can be an emergency.
self_care: Avoid the suspected trigger.
Do not scratch irritated skin.
Use medicines only as directed.
Track new foods, medicines, soaps, or exposures.
monitor: Facial, lip, tongue, or throat swelling
red_flags: Swelling of face, lips, tongue, or throat

title: Sore Throat Support
question: What can I do for a sore throat?
answer: A sore throat can come from viral infections, irritation, allergies, or strep throat. Fluids, rest, and soothing measures may help, but severe or persistent symptoms need medical guidance.
self_care: Drink warm fluids.
Gargle with warm salt water if safe for you.
Use throat lozenges only if age-appropriate.
Avoid smoke.
monitor: Symptoms lasting more than a few days

title: Nausea and Vomiting
question: What should I do for nausea or vomiting?
answer: Nausea and vomiting can happen with infections, food-related illness, medicines, pregnancy, migraine, or other causes. The biggest near-term risk is dehydration.
self_care: Pause solid food briefly if actively vomiting.
Take small sips of water or oral rehydration drink.
Return to bland foods as tolerated.
Avoid alcohol and greasy foods.

title: Diarrhea Basics
question: What can I do for diarrhea?
answer: Diarrhea is loose or watery stool. It often improves on its own, but fluids are important. Persistent diarrhea, blood, severe pain, or dehydration symptoms should be checked.
self_care: Drink water or oral rehydration solution.
Eat bland foods as tolerated.
Wash hands often.
Avoid alcohol and very fatty foods.
red_flags: Diarrhea in an infant or frail older adult

title: Urinary Symptoms
question: What should I know about urinary pain or frequency?
answer: Burning with urination, urgency, cloudy urine, or pelvic discomfort can happen with urinary tract irritation or infection. A clinician may need a urine test and treatment.
self_care: Drink fluids unless you were told to restrict them.
Avoid delaying urination.
Track fever, back pain, and symptoms.
Avoid starting leftover antibiotics.
monitor: Symptoms in men or older adults

title: Back Pain Basics
question: What can I do for back pain?
answer: Many mild back pain episodes improve with gentle movement, avoiding heavy lifting, heat or cold, and time. Sudden severe pain or nerve symptoms need medical attention.
self_care: Keep gentle activity as tolerated.
Avoid heavy lifting.
Try heat or cold if comfortable.
Use pain medicine only as directed.
red_flags: Loss of bladder or bowel control

title: Cuts and Wound Care
question: How should I care for a minor cut?
answer: Minor cuts can often be cleaned with running water and covered with a clean bandage. Deep, dirty, or animal bite wounds need medical guidance, and tetanus status may matter.
self_care: Wash hands before touching the wound.
Rinse with clean running water.
Apply gentle pressure for bleeding.
Cover with a clean bandage and change it daily.
red_flags: Wound from dirty or rusty object

title: Sleep Trouble
question: What can I try for insomnia?
answer: Trouble sleeping can be linked to stress, schedule changes, caffeine, medicines, pain, or health conditions. A regular routine and reducing stimulants can help.
self_care: Keep a consistent sleep and wake time.
Avoid caffeine late in the day.
Keep the bedroom dark and quiet.
Avoid screens close to bedtime if they worsen sleep.
red_flags: No sleep for several days with unusual behavior

title: Anxiety and Panic Feelings
question: What can I do when I feel anxious?
answer: Anxiety can cause racing thoughts, restlessness, fast heartbeat, sweating, or a feeling of panic. Slow breathing, grounding, and support can help in the moment, but ongoing or severe symptoms deserve care.
self_care: Try slow breathing.
Name five things you can see and four things you can feel.
Reduce caffeine if it worsens symptoms.
Reach out to a trusted person.
monitor: Symptoms that are new or severe

title: Eye Redness or Irritation
question: What should I know about red or irritated eyes?
answer: Eye redness can come from dryness, allergies, infection, or irritation. Pain, vision changes, injury, or chemical exposure needs urgent care.
self_care: Avoid rubbing the eye.
Wash hands often.
Use artificial tears if you already know they are safe for you.
Avoid sharing towels.
red_flags: Red eye with contact lens pain

title: Constipation
question: What can I do for constipation?
answer: Constipation can be related to low fiber, low fluids, inactivity, medicines, or health conditions. Gradual fiber, fluids, and movement may help.
self_care: Drink fluids unless restricted.
Add fiber gradually.
Move your body as tolerated.
Do not overuse laxatives without asking a clinician.
monitor: New constipation in an older adult

title: Chest Pain Safety
question: What should I do about chest pain?
answer: Chest pain can have many causes, but it can also be a warning sign of a heart or lung emergency. New, severe, crushing, or unexplained chest pain should be treated as urgent.
self_care: Stop activity and sit down.
Call local emergency services if symptoms are severe, new, or concerning.
Do not drive yourself to emergency care.
monitor: Pain spreading to arm, jaw, back, or neck
red_flags: Chest pain with shortness of breath
Pain spreading to arm, jaw, back, or neck

title: Stroke Warning Signs
question: What are stroke warning signs?
answer: Stroke warning signs can include face drooping, arm weakness, speech trouble, sudden confusion, sudden vision trouble, sudden dizziness, or sudden severe headache. Time matters.
self_care: Call local emergency services immediately if stroke is possible.
Note the time symptoms started.
Do not wait to see if symptoms go away.
